slides.title: outbox-md
slides.subtitle: Safe, async review for AI-generated Markdown specs

slides.title: The problem
bullets: AI agents generate big Markdown specs
Feedback is a disconnected chat message: "change section 3"

slides.title: The idea
bullets: Your comments never edit the document directly
They enter an ordered queue — the outbox
An AI agent works through them in order
You accept or reject — the doc is never corrupted

slides.title: Agent-agnostic
bullets: Ships no API keys, embeds no model
Any agent connects over MCP — Claude, GPT, Cursor

slides.title: Safe by design
bullets: Nothing you write is ever lost

slides.title: How to contribute
bullets: Good first issues, clean seams to build on
Council, dashboard, retrieval — all designed in
Sign your commits (DCO), open a PR

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Welcome back. Today we're looking at outbox-md. Ava, what is it?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: It's a local-first tool for reviewing the Markdown specs that AI agents generate — safely, and asynchronously.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: What's wrong with how we do that today?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Today you read a long spec in a chat window, and the only feedback you can give is a disconnected message like change section three. Re-prompting often rewrites unrelated parts, with no ordering and no audit trail.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: So what does outbox-md do differently?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Your comments never edit the document directly. They go into an ordered queue called the outbox. An agent works through them one at a time and proposes changes you accept or reject, so the document is never corrupted.

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: You highlight text and leave a comment. It lands in the outbox. A connected agent claims it, proposes an edit, and you accept — the file is rewritten, and every other comment re-anchors to follow its text.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Which AI does the actual work?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Any of them. outbox-md ships no API keys and embeds no model. It's a substrate that any agent — Claude, GPT, Cursor — connects to over MCP, and it runs locally in one Docker container.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Why go to all this trouble instead of just editing the file?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Because the whole point is safe iteration. Everything is ordered and auditable, changes are suggestions you approve, and nothing you write is ever lost.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: It's open source — how can people help?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: It's MIT-licensed and public. There are good first issues, and clean seams for big features like the AI council, the dashboard, and retrieval. Sign your commits, open a pull request, and you're in.

lines.speaker: Andrew
lines.text: Love it. Where do we start?

lines.speaker: Ava
lines.text: Head to the repo, give it a star, and try the quickstart. The links are right below.
